topics.jca: The Joint Copyright Assignment (JCA) requirement forced developers contributing to OpenOffice.org to assign joint copyright to Sun Microsystems. This allowed Sun to relicense the codebase and create proprietary derivatives without giving community contributors reciprocal governance rights.
topics.oracle: Oracle acquired Sun Microsystems for $7.4 billion in January 2010. By August 2010, Oracle sued Google over Java, demonstrating a willingness to weaponize intellectual property, triggering a crisis of trust in the OpenOffice.org community.
topics.forking: The Document Foundation chose to fork OpenOffice.org to create LibreOffice. This was deemed the only response consistent with free software values. LibreOffice grew to over 200 million users, vindicating the community's choice.
topics.mariadb: Simultaneous to the LibreOffice fork, MySQL's creator forked it to create MariaDB for almost exactly the same reasons following the Oracle acquisition. This revealed a structural incompatibility between extractive corporate ownership and open source community health.
topics.stewardship: A steward holds resources in trust for a community, limited by the purposes of that trust. An owner holds resources for their own benefit with unlimited legal authority. Sun acted as an owner legally, but a steward practically. Oracle acted strictly as an owner.
topics.apache: Instead of engaging with The Document Foundation, Oracle eventually donated the depleting OpenOffice codebase to the Apache Software Foundation. Today, Apache OpenOffice has a fraction of LibreOffice's active contributor base.

timeline.event: Sun Microsystems acquires Star Division and open-sources StarOffice as OpenOffice.org.
OpenOffice.org download counts run into the tens of millions, though tensions over the JCA brew.
Oracle Corporation completes its $7.4 billion acquisition of Sun Microsystems.
Oracle files a lawsuit against Google regarding Java patents, signaling an aggressive IP posture.
The Document Foundation is announced, officially forking the project into LibreOffice.
Oracle donates the remaining OpenOffice.org codebase to the Apache Software Foundation.

quiz.question: What was the structural flaw baked into OpenOffice.org from the beginning?
options: A) A lack of community contributors

quiz.question: Which company's acquisition of Sun Microsystems triggered the creation of LibreOffice?
According to the case study, what happens when a community forks a project?
options: A) It is always an admission of failure.
B) It is a structural guarantee that keeps governance actors accountable.
C) The code automatically becomes proprietary.

quiz.question: What was the eventual fate of OpenOffice.org under Oracle's governance?
options: A) It was donated to the Apache Software Foundation.
B) It became the most popular office suite in the world.
C) It was sold to Google.

quiz.question: Which database project forked from Oracle ownership at the same time as LibreOffice?
